data.title: Weak Earnings Forecasts Weigh On Market Outlook For 2025
data.description:
Earnings season kicks off this week, and it will be a very important earnings season, especially given the high levels of uncertainty surrounding the economy. The general trend for earnings has been that they have been coming down in recent months, and this has been one of the driving forces behind why the stock market has struggled in 2025.

If those estimates continue to struggle, they will continue to weigh on the market going forward, as they have already. If earnings guidance changes or is followed by further downgrades, it will have a pronounced impact on expected growth and, more importantly, the multiple investors are willing to pay for stocks.

Since September, earnings estimates have slipped to $264.21 per share from around $274.03 per share, a decline of about 4%, which accounts for some of the S&P 500 decline in 2025. Falling earnings estimates have sent the expected growth rate to just 11.2% from around 15%. Over time, sales estimates have also declined to $1,995 per share from $2008 per share, with growth slipping to 5.1% from 5.8%.

Additionally, we have seen the number of companies with negative earnings revisions rising and those with positive earnings revisions falling. With earnings mostly being revised lower and slower growth expected, it would make sense to see an index that has been struggling since the beginning of the year.

Margins Are Too High

The one aspect that seems not to have been negatively affected is margins. While they have fallen somewhat, they are still historically very high at 13.2%, which is more than 70 bps higher than 2024 and more than 30 bps higher than the previous recording in 2021, at 12.9%. This would seem to suggest that earnings are still at risk of falling further from their current levels. Historically, margins tend to start high and come down over time.

So while some of the recent market declines may have been tariff-related, it seems possible that the broader declines we saw before the tariff-related news were due to the underlying weakness in earnings forecasts. Assuming margins fall back to 2024 levels at 12.5%, it implies that 2025 earnings will fall to $249.50. That would be positive growth over 2024, but estimates would be about 5% lower than current levels.

2026 In Doubt

It also appears that estimates for 2026 would probably need to be adjusted lower, as those margins are currently sitting at more than 14%. Earnings are currently expected to rise to around $302 per share, but again, assuming margins that are closer to 2024-type levels. Then, earnings estimates for 2026 would need to decline to around $265, which is where the current 2025 earnings estimates are.

As we go through earnings season starting this week, some of these questions will begin to clear up and give a perfect sense of where the market is heading because markets generally trade with earnings, with sentiment, and growth rates the drivers of the multiples the market assigns.

So while the tariff war may be on pause for now, the underlying issues in the market may not.

data.title: Here Is A Solid Investment TIP
data.description:
At the beginning of February, I shared my positive perspective on Treasury Inflation-Protected Securities ("TIPS"), giving 15+ Year U.S. TIPS Index Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (LTPZ) a Strong Buy. Since that piece was published, LTPZ has dropped by 4% (with a return including dividends of -3.2%). I still like LTPZ a lot and own it in my IRA and in my trading account, but the trade idea has improved since then due to the tariff turmoil. I discussed the very large iShares TIPS Bond ETF (NYSEARCA:TIP) in that piece too, and it has been unchanged in price (+0.6% return with dividend). Today, I explain again what TIPS are, and I give TIP a Strong Buy as I discuss the risks and the opportunities.

The Current Tariff Turmoil

When Donald Trump was President the first time, he raised tariffs on China in response to Chinese theft of U.S. intellectual property. The two countries inched close to a trade war.

During the 2024 election, he discussed tariffs as a response to the drug trade and illegal immigration. After he won the Presidential election, he suggested tariffs imposed upon Canada and Mexico, as well as on Chinese goods.

In February, Trump shared a fact sheet on tariffs for these three countries. He ended up delaying implementation. In early April, the world was shocked by the actual proposed tariffs and stocks tanked. Trump delayed most of the tariffs again on 4/09 (for 90 days). A 10% tariff was maintained against almost all imports. The China situation has escalated.

Trump seems very set on tariffs, despite many of his supporters and most of his opponents fearing inflationary effects. The Federal Reserve Chairman, Jerome Powell, shared some comments that suggest that the Fed is in a tough spot.

Turning to monetary policy, we face a highly uncertain outlook with elevated risks of both higher unemployment and higher inflation. The new Administration is in the process of implementing substantial policy changes in four distinct areas: trade, immigration, fiscal policy, and regulation.

It's been a long, long time since the world was ramping up tariffs, and investors are highly concerned. Right now, it is a challenge for the U.S. government, which has a massive debt and must issue more debt (U.S. Treasury securities) as its existing debt matures.

Bear Market or Bull Market?

Gold has soared in 2025, and it has been strong for quite some time. In the piece I wrote on LTPZ, I shared a chart of SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) since its inception. Up now 23% in 2025 to a new all-time high, it has advanced a lot since that piece two months ago:

Gold is in a bull market! Perhaps gold, long viewed as a hedge against inflation, is benefitting from the pickup in inflation fears, or perhaps it is benefitting from a flight to quality. There are no countries in the world that have a "gold standard" any longer, though central banks do traffic in gold holdings. Gold has outpaced silver and other precious metals this year, as well as oil. Over the past decade, it has outpaced them, too.

Bonds were volatile last week, but are not that changed since year-end. Here are Treasury yields since the elections and year-end:

So, rates are generally lower since year-end, with the curve steeper. Since the elections, most Treasuries have increased in yield as the yield curve has steepened substantially. During this time, Fed Funds has been reduced by 0.75%. The 2-year Treasury and the 5-year Treasury yield less than Fed Funds, which is targeted at 4.25-4.50%.

Stocks seem like they are in a bear market. Most investors believe that a 20% decline in an index represents a bear market, and the S&P 500 traded below that level on Monday. The S&P 500 is currently down just 8.5% in price year-to-date. The Russell 2000 index is currently down 16.3% year-to-date, and it is 19.5% below its peak earlier this year. It traded more than 20% below its peak last week.

The iShares TIPS Bond ETF

In that last piece, I shared the history of TIPS, which was introduced in 1997. There is over $2 trillion outstanding, which is less than 10% of the outstanding U.S. federal debt. I also explained how TIPS protect against inflation.

Unlike other securities the Treasury issues, the TIPS principal is not fixed. The security was offered by the Treasury to appeal to investors concerned with inflation, as that can have a terrible impact on the prices of Treasury securities with long maturities. The interest-rate TIPS pays tends to be lower than regular Treasury yields, but the maturity amount is tied to inflation, through the Consumer Price Index from the Bureau of Labor Statistics. It provides a daily index ratio that helps investors to see how it is changing.

Many investors hold the TIPS themselves, but there are several large ETFs, including TIP, which currently has $14 billion in net assets. It holds 50 securities with maturities from 2026 to 2055 and an expense ratio of just 0.18%. It traded 2.9 million shares on Friday, which is a bit higher than its recent average daily trading and was about $313 million of trading value.

In 2025, TIP, with a total return of 2.0%, is exceeding cash returns to date slightly. Looking at it over the past five years along with two other ETFs, LTPZ and the iShares 0-5 Year TIPS Bond ETF (STIP), TIP has returned less than a money-market fund:

Comparing TIP with iShares U.S. Treasury Bond ETF (GOVT), a large fund run by the same company with an effective duration (5.67) that is similar to the TIP effective duration of 6.41, TIP has outpaced GOVT slightly year-to-date. Over the past decade, TIP has returned slightly more than cash, but it has far outpaced GOVT:

This return advantage is due mainly to the increased principal payments, which were boosted after the pandemic. Here is the price return of these two iShares ETFs:

TIPS and TIP ETF were a bit overheated in late 2021. Since the end of that year, TIP has declined slightly more in price than GOVT. Its total return, though, has been better at -5.6% versus -7.7%.

The TIP Chart

Over the last year, the price of TIP, which posted a 52-week high early in the month, has increased. There was a gap down in price on 4/7:

There is also a gap that remains open from January, down to 106.30. These gaps don't necessarily have to get filled, but the gap was very successfully tested on 4/11, when the ETF reversed in price to end slightly higher after gapping down and trading lower at first.

The returns of TIP will be highly dependent upon future CPI, as the interest payments are tied to that measure. To me, TIP seemed overdone earlier this month in the short term, but it could get back to $111 over time. In fact, I think that the price could do better. It traded to $131 in 2021, which was perhaps too high at the time. Here is the chart over the past few years:

I see several things in this long-term chart. First, there is a big gap above to about 114 that was created in September 2022. Second, the volume has picked up recently, highlighting potential investor interest. Third, the volume is still a lot lower than when the sell-off started in early 2022. Fourth, this looks like a reverse head-and shoulders formation.

Again, the total return ahead will depend on inflation. As far as thinking about the price over the next year, I think that it could advance by 15-20% to $124-130, which would leave it below the all-time high price from 2022.

This large, liquid monthly paying ETF states that its effective duration currently is 6.42 on its website, so this is effectively about a 3% decline in yield. Is that likely? Well, during the pandemic, the yields were slightly below 1% according to the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. And, they were almost -1% in 2013. So, if inflation concerns do pick up, then the TIPS could drop in yield. TIP would rally tremendously. Those who accept this outlook should also consider buying LTPZ, which could advance more.

Risks with TIPS

TIPS have risks that I discussed in that piece in February and repeat here:

The biggest risk to TIPS is that inflation declines more than expected. Taking a lower yield to protect against inflation works out to be a wasted insurance policy. If inflation falls to 1% and the market reprices so that TIPS are offered at spreads of -1% relative to similar maturity Treasury securities, owners will miss out on big gains in price and get paid less at maturity. The current 10-year Treasury yield at 4.54% could drop to 2.50%, which would put its price up about 15%. The 10-year TIPS would increase by about 6%. The total return differential would be even greater, since the TIPS yield less than regular Treasuries. Of course, negative inflation would be even more harmful, as the principal value could decline.

Another risk to TIPS is that they are U.S. Treasury obligations, and they could get downgraded along with all Treasury securities. Investors in TIPS and regular Treasuries are taking debt from an entity that is highly regarded now but could face pressure due to the high debt.

This second risk seems bigger now than it did in February. TIP shares the same risks as TIPS, which it holds.

Trade Ideas

For those who like TIPS already and invest in them either directly or through an ETF or fund, extending durations makes sense in my view. The inflation-protection that the securities offer through higher yields if inflation kicks in is the same, but the longer maturities will perform better if investors begin to increase their interest. Here are the current yields according to Bloomberg:

Investors need to remember that the yields do not include future adjustments for inflation. On this basis, the 5-year TIPS yields 2.43% less than the 5-year Treasury. The 10-year yields 2.24% less, while the 30-year yields 2.19% less. Assuming 3% inflation, the yields for TIPS would be higher than Treasuries. If inflation ends up being higher, the returns would be even greater.

Over the last month, these yields have all increased more than the yields on the similar-maturity Treasuries. Over the past year, the 5-year TIPS yield has declined more than the 5-year Treasury, while the 10-year and 30-year TIPS yields have increased by more than the Treasuries.

So, the conclusion for those who want TIPS for their inflation protection is to extend maturities. For those invested in shorter funds or ETFs, this means buying TIP or even LTPZ.

I have been bearish on Treasuries for many reasons, and, for those who are concerned about inflation, it makes sense to sell Treasuries and buy TIPS in my view. Investors can exit Treasuries and buy TIPS or funds and buy TIPS funds, like TIP.

My final idea is, for those who hold gold due to inflation fears, TIP makes more sense. Gold, as measured by GLD ETF, has soared this year. Here is the long-term perspective of GLD and TIP:

I am actually short GLD through owning a leveraged inverse ETF, ProShares UltraShort Gold (GLL).

Conclusion

The financial markets are in turmoil, and I think the current situation bodes well for TIPS and the TIP ETF. I shared a price return for year-end of 15-20% higher, and the total return, which includes the interest payments and the potential principal adjustments, could be higher. Gold is soaring, the Federal Reserve has its hands tied, and inflation could reaccelerate.

Stocks are falling, but this bear market, the first since the one that ended in March 2009, may just be starting. Bond yields are up a bit for longer maturities since year-end, though down for shorter maturities.

Cash, which yields close to Fed Funds, seems attractive relative to Treasuries, and if inflation accelerates, it will perform better. But cash will likely underperform TIPS and the TIP ETF.

data.title: The Allstate Corporation: You Are In Good Hands
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I would say there isn't any doubt that The Allstate Corporation (ALL) truly lives up to its mantra, "You are in good hands." Since the financial markets started plummeting in early February, I have keenly been watching Allstate Corp.'s stock performance. One of the things that I noticed was that the stock was not affected as much as its peers, as shown below. It's like ALL had a pause moment and didn't react to the global panic, tariff news and the reciprocal tariffs imposed globally. Yet, before all this turmoil, the stock was doing pretty well. It had solid growth prospects, its earnings were looking promising, and it had quite a decent combined ratio.

As the announced tariffs that came into effect in April caused global financial markets to tumble, ALL stock fell to $180 per share. However, on 9th April, the White House reported that President Trump had issued a 90-day pause on tariffs and ALL immediately saw signs of a turnaround. It rose to $193 and it's one of the reasons why I definitely think the timing is great for The Allstate Corporation.

In my previous articles on Mercury, Skyward, Fidelis and IGIC where I analyzed specialty insurance companies, one of the key factors that I keenly look for is a profitable insurance business model and for sure ALL is no exception. In their recent earnings release, the property liability combined ratio increased from 91.2% in 2023 to 84.6%. This meant that for every $1.0 collected as premium, $0.84 was used for expenses and claims, leaving the company with $0.16 as profit before investment income. The protection homeowners segment had a combined ratio of 67.5% in 2023 and 62.5% in 2024. The CEO attributed this rise to an increase in premiums.

Property-Liability combined ratio was 86.9 for the quarter, which was 2.6 points better than the prior year, primarily due to higher average earned premiums and improved loss experience more than offsetting increased advertising investment and higher catastrophe losses.

In previous articles on specialty insurance companies, I have always had a bias towards the adjusted combined ratio and its importance in evaluating an insurer's profitability. This is because the ratio is the pillar of underwriting profitability. Given ALL has had a strong combined ratio across all their major businesses, I am confident in recommending ALL as a strong Buy.

The Catalyst

The Allstate Corporation announced its sale of its Health and Benefit business to Nationwide during its 4Q24 earnings release. This sale is expected to bring in $3.25Bn in proceeds and as a result, it will generate a financial book of $1.0 Bn in 2025. In this regard, I would expect the following:

  • The $1.0 Bn will increase the net income in 2025. This will be shown as a single item in the statement of financial performance. However, from a non-GAAP perspective, the $1.0 Bn might still inflate the revenues but won't count towards adjusted net earnings.
  • The transaction will increase the retained earnings and as a result, increase book value per share by $3.77 per share. This is under the assumption that shares outstanding remain at 265Mn.
  • By the end of 2025, the company will have a total of $21.9 Bn in available capital. This should give the company some dry powder to pursue other activities such as the shares repurchase program and other investments as noted by the CEO.

Growth

SA Quant ratings have The Allstate Corporation growth prospects at A+ growth grade. This growth is as a result of its organic and sustainable growth of 12.28% which is above the sector median of 5.91%. Additionally, in the last 5 years, ALL's average growth rate has been 9.19% which, I believe, is sustainable and consistent.

Profitability

In terms of profitability, SA Quant ratings have ALL's profitability grade at A+ profitability grade. As we previously observed, its combined ratio is within profitability levels. With most of their business segments operating under 85%, it's clear that ALL strives to run at profitability despite experiencing a couple of catastrophic losses in 2024. Additionally, the sale of its Health and Benefits business is a testament to moving the core business from struggling areas to profitable business lines while maximizing shareholder value, as mentioned by the CEO:

The decision to maximize shareholder value by combining the Health and Benefits businesses with companies that have strategic alignment also was successful with two of the three businesses now under contract to be sold for $3.25 billion

Valuation

In regard to valuation, ALL trades at a forward EV/Sales of 0.88x, which represents a 69.82% discount against the sector median. Additionally, ALL is trading at a forward price/sales of 0.74x, which is a 71.13% discount to its sector median.

In comparison with its peers, ALL's EV/sales stand at 0.95x, while American International Group (AIG) trades at 2.13x, The Travelers (TRV) trades at 1.38x, Tokio Marine Holdings (OTCPK:TKOMY) trades at 1.11x, and Intact Financial Corp (OTCPK:IFCZF) and Arch Capital Group (ACGL) trade at 1.96x and 2.12x, respectively.

Allstate's Key Risk

One of the biggest risks I see with ALL is that the company may not be in a position to complete the sale of its Health & Benefit business segment. This will definitely hurt their projected financials in 2025, which could, in turn, affect their multi-year transformative strategy. Failure to dispose of these assets will still have an exposure in the financial book, as the operating results will continue to be reported in ALL's books as part of net income until completely sold off. Until all standard closing conditions are met, including regulatory approvals, the health business will still be under Allstate and will continue weighing in on its balance sheet.

There is also another risk that ALL could experience large catastrophic events. In 2024, ALL reported catastrophic losses worth $4.96 Bn, though the company has strict risk management controls and loss management experience. These CAT losses were attributed to LA wildfires, hurricanes, and severe storms. However, with ALL's top-notch risk and return management, the company seems to have well-defined risk modeling, as noted by the CEO:

Homeowners insurance generated $1.3 billion of underwriting income for the year while covering $3.7 billion of customers' catastrophe losses, reflecting an industry-leading business model. We rapidly supported customers impacted by the January California wildfires and related losses are expected to be about $1.1 billion, pre-tax, net of reinsurance, reflecting a decision to reduce market share beginning in 2007 and a comprehensive reinsurance program.

Final Thoughts

As mentioned earlier, what stood out most with ALL, was its performance between February and April 2025. As markets and stocks were plummeting due to panic, tariffs, and fears of a recession, ALL stood strong. While this tends to be the case with large insurance companies, I believe there is something special about Allstate. And as we have seen, ALL is shifting from non-performing business to profitable business lines. The adjusted combined ratios for their property-liability business improved to 84.6%, protection auto improved to 93.4% from 99.9%, and protection homeowners improved from 67.3% to 62.5%. This clearly explains its profitability grade, growth prospects, and the valuation discount. In any insurance company, the largest risk is catastrophic losses, and as we have observed, ALL is in control of its risk management framework, even with the 2024 $4.96 Bn catastrophic payout. This is a strong company with consistent profits, reliable growth prospects, and an intentional effort to generate shareholder value.

data.title: SCHY: A Solid 4%-Yielding Non-U.S. Dividend ETF
data.description:
The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) is a potentially attractive investment option for investors that want to build equity exposure outside of the U.S. The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF has a much shorter performance history than the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) and also manages significantly less capital.

However, the ETF could be an attractive dividend option for investors that want to diversify into the best dividend stocks outside of the U.S. market: the SCHY specifically screens for non-U.S. dividend stocks that have paid a dividend for at least 10 years, ensuring that only the most predictable dividend-paying companies are included in the ETF's portfolio.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF also outperformed Schwab's larger U.S.-focused ETF in terms of dividend growth and has a higher initial yield offer. With a competitive expense ratio, I believe the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could grow into a much larger ETF going forward.

Previous rating

While I didn’t cover the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF before, I have had a strong buy rating on the U.S. version of Schwab’s dividend ETF: Why I Am Aggressively Buying The Drop. The main reason I liked the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF is that the ETF has delivered double-digit annual NAV returns since its inception and has grown its dividend for a long time as well.

Because of this, the SCHD has been a solid compounding machine for dividend investors, although its dividend growth has slowed in recent years. Investors that want, for diversification reasons, to add an ETF focused on large-cap, high-yielding non-U.S. stocks to their portfolios may want to take a look at the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF.

Strong diversification profile, portfolio split

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF pales in comparison to its bigger cousin, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, in terms of size: the SCHY manages $844M in net assets compared to $66.6B for the SCHD. Both ETFs offer investors the opportunity to invest in dividend stocks from companies that have focused on growing their dividends with the major difference being the geographic orientation of the fund. Both ETFs also offer comparable expense ratios which helps investors compound their invested capital quicker: the SCHY has an expense ratio of 0.08% compared to 0.06% for SCHD.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is focused on large-cap multinationals and has exposure to Europe, Australia, Asia and Latin America. The three biggest countries by investment destination were the United Kingdom (15%), Australia (12%) and France (11%). The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, on the other hand, only owns U.S. stocks.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF owns a diversified portfolio of dividend-paying stocks whose names include leading non-U.S. consumer, energy and pharmaceutical companies. The biggest holding for the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is ENEL, an Italy-based manufacturer and distributor of energy and gas.

This company currently has a portfolio weighting of 4.42% which is followed by Ono Pharmaceutical with a weighting of 4.40% and Unilever with a representation of 4.21%. As you can see in the portfolio split below, the top ten holdings are of fairly equal size, making the SCHY a highly attractive investment option for investors seeking diversification (both in geographic terms, as well as, in terms of portfolio size).

In my opinion, the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF can be expected to do well, in terms of both net asset value and dividend growth, as long as the European economy is growing. The top three industries, with an about equal ~15% weighting are Financials, Consumer Staples and Communication Services.

NAV returns compared: SCHD vs. SCHY

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF was launched on April 29, 2021, which means the ETF has a relatively short investment history. For comparison, The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF was launched on October 20, 2011 and has therefore a much more time-tested (and longer) performance track record. The SCHY delivered an annual NAV return of 3.97% since its inception about four years ago, while the SCHD is looking much better, mainly due to the outperformance of the U.S. stock market in the last decade: the SCHD returned 12.93% annually since 2011.

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF under-performed its larger ETF cousin over its relatively short period of existence. In the longer term, the Schwab U.S. Dividend ETF has delivered very strong NAV results and has proven itself as a very capable wealth-building vehicle. Since its inception in 2011, the SCHD has delivered a total NAV return of 200% which makes the ETF an attractive long-term investment option or investors that want to bet on the U.S. market. For investors that want international exposure, the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could be an attractive investment vehicle, especially given the ETF's strong screening criteria, promising NAV profile and low expense ratio).

SCHY offers a slightly better yield

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is currently trading at an initial dividend yield of 4.38% which compares against a yield of 4.16% for the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF.

Risks with SCHY

The biggest risk for the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF is that the ETF may not grow its net asset value as fast as the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF and therefore lose out with investors. The SCHD has delivered double-digit NAV returns since it was launched in 2011. If the SCHY fails to achieve higher net asset value returns going forward, the ETF may lose fund assets.

Final thoughts

The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF could be an interesting addition to a dividend investor’s portfolio, especially if said investor is looking for diversification outside of the U.S. market. The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF owns some of the biggest European energy, pharmaceutical and consumer stocks, all of which have a history of growing their dividends. While the Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF does not have as long a record as its larger U.S. cousin, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, the ETF has a ton of potential to grow its dividends long term. Additionally, the yield looks good (4%+) and the expense ratio is low. In my opinion, dividend-focused retirees and conservative investors may find value in the ETF's different geographic positioning that provides a hedge against a major downturn in the U.S. market.

data.title: Snowflake: It Is Time To Buy Tech Again (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
It is time to warm back up to Snowflake (SNOW). The company appears to be at an inflection in growth rates, with management confident that net retention rates may be stabilizing. Importantly, the valuation is such that one does not need an acceleration in top-line growth for the story to work out. While SNOW admittedly trades at a relative premium to peers, I expect this premium to persist given the attractive secular story. As the company executes against expanding profit margins, I expect the stock to deliver strong forward returns. I am upgrading the stock to a buy rating.

SNOW Stock Price

I last covered SNOW in January, when I downgraded the stock due to both valuation, as well, as my deteriorating view of the growth story. The stock has declined around 11% since then.

There are cheaper names to be found in software as the entire sector has re-rated downward, but SNOW nonetheless looks attractive here.

SNOW Stock Key Metrics

SNOW is an enterprise tech company best known as a data warehouse, helping its customers to organize and get the most value from their data. The company was a very popular stock ever since it came public based on the “growth of data” story and that became even more relevant following the rise of generative AI, but the company has failed to show a sustained acceleration in revenue growth rates. I suspect that many investors may be now losing faith that generative AI may be the tailwinds that they had been hoping for.

In the most recent quarter, SNOW delivered 28% YoY product revenue growth to $943 million, demolishing guidance of between $906 million and $911 million. I note that management typically aims for an accurate guidance target, making that outperformance quite notable.

The company saw current remaining performance obligations grow 27% in the quarter, representing a strong result but decelerating sequentially from the 36% number posted in the third quarter. I have previously viewed cRPO growth as being a leading indicator for revenue growth, but am no longer of this view given what I have seen in the sector over the past few years.

SNOW saw its net revenue retention rate decelerate by a hair to 126%. On the conference call, management noted that they do not believe that it will drop to 124% or 125% but should stabilize “in the mid-120s.”

On a slightly sour note, SNOW is one of the few software names in my coverage universe that has not been making consistent progress on profitability. The company’s GAAP operating loss has somehow expanded this year. It is possible that management is waiting for revenue growth rates to decelerate more meaningfully before turning the gears on profitability.

SNOW ended the quarter with $5.3 billion of cash and investments versus $2.3 billion of debt, representing a solid net cash balance sheet.

Looking ahead, management has guided for the first quarter to see up to 22% YoY revenue growth to between $955 million and $960 million with 5% non-GAAP operating margins. Management has guided the full year to see around 24% product revenue growth.

Management outlined expectations for the second half to “show more muster” due to new products. Management commented that they are “now seeing Iceberg as more of a tailwind” as it is “opening up so much more data for us.” Investors (including yours truly) had previously been worried that the proliferation of Iceberg tables might be a material headwind, but management noted that they have “yet to see massive amounts of data move out of Snowflake.”

Is SNOW Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

As of recent prices, SNOW traded hands at just around 11x forward sales.

I should first note that many software names have sold off in this quarter, and those looking for cheaper value might be advised to look elsewhere. Yet, SNOW has a compelling thesis here. The company’s exposure to the growth of data will arguably be an attractive story for years to come. While the market uncertainty created by tariffs might lead to reduced demand for IT spending over the next year, I do not see any material impact on the long-term thesis. I can see this name sustaining at least a 10x sales multiple, if not higher. The company might reach 30% net margins over the long term - 10x sales equates to around 33x earnings. With the company projected to sustain 18% to 22% revenue growth over the next many years, the stock might deliver annual returns of around 16% to 18% after accounting for ongoing operating losses and mild multiple compression. That looks like an attractive return proposition from a leader in enterprise tech.

SNOW Stock Risks

It is possible that demand for enterprise tech and SNOW declines due to macro pressures. It is possible that generative AI will have a disruptive effect on the business. I have noted that the stock is not relative to peers. This, plus the company’s lack of strong profit margins, may make the stock more exposed to volatility in the event of a market downturn (with the tariff war in full swing, this may be an underappreciated risk). I note that it is not entirely clear if the company has superior products than competitors like Databricks.

SNOW Stock Conclusion

It is finally time to be buying tech again, and SNOW looks attractive here after the selloff. The company has a net cash balance sheet and continues to sustain aggressive top-line growth rates. I see a clear path to market-beating returns from here, though we may see some volatility in the near term. SNOW shares many of the characteristics that I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I am upgrading SNOW to a buy rating.

data.title: RQI: 8% Yield, 5% Discount Contrarian Play
data.description:
The US 10-year Treasury rate jumped during this past week's tariffs turmoil, back up to 4.40%, after drifting down to ~4%, vs. ~4.75% in early 2025:

That spike wasn't great news for real estate, as the 10-year Treasury yield and mortgage rates tend to move in tandem. The Fed has taken a wait-and-see approach to future rate cuts, so no joy from that corner either.

But, taking a contrarian view. What if the tariffs throw the US into a recession? Will the Fed be forced to come to the rescue, with the well-known “Fed put” of the past?

Those of you with that view may want to take a look at the Cohen & Steers Quality Income Realty Fund (NYSE:RQI), which can offer you exposure to real estate assets. RQI is a closed-end fund.

Fund Profile:

"The primary investment objective of the Fund is high current income through investment in real estate securities. The secondary investment objective is capital appreciation. Under normal market conditions, at least 80% of the Fund's total assets are invested in income-producing common stocks and other securities issued by real estate companies, such as real estate investment trusts (REITs). Real estate securities include common stocks, preferred stocks, and other equity and debt securities issued by real estate companies, including REITs and similar REIT-like entities." (RQI site)

RQI began in February 2002. With $1.6B in net assets, it's in the top 15 largest Equity CEFs, 384K in average daily volume, and 208 holdings. Management uses leverage, which was at 30.48%, as of 4/9/25. The expense ratio is 3.78%, which includes 2.40% in interest expense:

Holdings:

RQI's top real estate sub-sectors include telecommunications at 13%, healthcare at 12%, and data centers at 10%. It also holds 12% in corporate bonds; for a total of 47% of its portfolio. The portfolio looks diversified within the real estate sector, with seven other sub-sectors comprising 34%, and other assets comprising the remaining 18%:

Healthcare REIT Welltower (WELL) is RQI's top holding at 8.4%, followed by American Tower (AMT) at 7.7%, and Digital Realty (DLR) at 6.1%. The remaining seven top positions range from Apartment REIT UDR at 2.9% to Prologis at 4.8%:

The US is RQI's biggest regional exposure, at 78.93%, vs. 80% for its index, the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs Index. RQI is underweight 2.44% in North America and overweight 2.95% in Europe, compared to the index:

Dividends:

RQI has paid a monthly $.08 distribution since 2015. At its 4/10/25 price of $11.22, RQI yielded 8.56%.

NII fell 23% in 2024, to $21.89M, while net realized gains ballooned, to $173.83M, vs. $43.84M in 2023. Unrealized appreciation, however, swung from a $162.79M gain in 2023, to -$97.1M in 2024.

Net assets increased by $234.94M in 2023, vs. $98.6M in 2024:

Taxes:

Management estimates that Q1 2025 distributions were comprised mostly long-term gains with no return of capital.

Performance:

While RQI lagged the S&P and the broad real estate sector over the past year, its 8.5% yield gave it a higher total one-year return of 3.6%, vs. 2.7% for the S&P, and 1.4% for XLRE.

As of 4/10/25, RQI has held up a bit better so far in 2025 than the S&P, but has lagged the broad real estate sector:

As of 12/31/24, RQI's 11.58% price return since inception was over 300 basis points better than its blended benchmark index and ~270 basis points better than its linked index.

RQI's three-year returns suffered from a very difficult year in 2022, when rates were rising. It fell 31% in price and 27% in NAV. Its five- and 10-year returns outperformed its indexes:

Looking at RQI's top 10 performance reveals some winners and losers so far in volatile 2025.

Two out of four of 2025's top performers are telecom infrastructure companies American Tower (AMT) and Crown Castle (CCI). Healthcare REIT Welltower and Casino REIT VICI are the other two positive performers so far in 2025.

The rest of RQI's top 10 have logged negative returns so far in 2025, with data centers Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Realty (DLR) being the biggest losers, both down ~18%:

Risks:

Concentration Risk: While RQI is diversified within real estate, its sector focus can expose investors to concentration risk, such as in 2022, when rising rates caused certain sectors to pull back.

Market Risk: CEFs often trade at a discount from their net asset value, giving them a risk that stock prices overall will decline over long or short periods, which could cause investors to lose principal.

Valuations:

Buying CEFs at deeper than historic discounts to NAV can be a useful strategy due to mean reversion. CEFs' daily valuations are calculated at the end of each trading day. As of the 4/9/25 close, RQI was trading at a 5.56% discount to its NAV of $12.05. That discount is deeper than its one-, three-, and five-year discounts of 4.21%, 4.83% and 4.94%, respectively:

Parting Thoughts:

RQI is ~8% above its 52-week low, and is hovering near its oversold line on its slow stochastic chart.

We rate RQI a speculative Buy based upon the bet that the Fed will lower rates in 2025. But don't bet the ranch.

All tables furnished by Hidden Dividend Stocks Plus, unless otherwise noted

data.title: Taiwan Semiconductor Earnings Preview: Despite Risks And Headwinds, Valuation Remains Attractive
data.description:
Taiwan Semiconductor is one of the best-managed companies and important companies in the world. - Warren Buffett

We have been admirers of Taiwan Semiconductor (NYSE:TSM) even before the market caught AI fever. We agree with Warren Buffett that it is probably one of the most important companies in the world, and probably one of the most interesting as well. Unfortunately, there is some idiosyncratic risk, which Buffett made clear when he expressed admiration for Taiwan Semiconductor, but still sold Berkshire Hathaway's (BRK.A)(BRK.B) multi-billion dollar position in TSMC because "he did not like the location".

While we acknowledge the risk highlighted by Buffett, we believe a small position in TSMC can still be justified if the valuation is right for investors with a higher risk tolerance. It is important to mention that should TSMC's chip production be disrupted, the effect would also be catastrophic for many of its customers such as NVIDIA (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL).

It will be interesting to listen to the upcoming earnings call on April 17th, in particular management's take on the potential impact from tariffs and the increased risk of a recession in the U.S. In the meantime, as TSMC reports monthly sales we already know that Q1 revenue slightly exceeded analyst estimates. Revenue of NT$839.25 billion, or ~$25.6 billion, was an increase of roughly 41.6% year-over-year. This is impressive, although some could be pull-forward demand as a result of tariff uncertainty. We will have to wait until the earnings report to see if the full-year sales target is revised given recent developments, and if a weakening global outlook for chip demand results in a revised capital expenditures plan.

Technology Leader

While semiconductor manufacturing equipment makers like ASML (ASML) get a lot of credit for recent chip advances, and rightly so, optimizing the use of these machines is a technical feat as well. In some ways, companies like ASML and Applied Materials (AMAT) are the oven and kitchen utensils manufacturers, while TSMC is the Master Chef that figures out the optimal way to use these tools to produce the best results.

Given the computing power and energy efficiency benefits from smaller transistors, it is no surprise that many customers want their chips manufactured on the leading edge, which currently means 5nm and 3nm semiconductor nodes, for which there appears to be an insatiable demand. Importantly, Taiwan Semiconductor is not resting on its laurels, instead it is working to maintain or widen its lead over the competition. For example, during the Q4 earnings call, management highlighted that they are on track to start producing the 2nm (N2) node in the second half of 2025. This scale down is expected to result in a more than 10x speed improvement at the same power, or a 20% to 30% power improvement at constant speed. This should be particularly attractive for AI data center chips, which are already struggling to procure enough electricity.

Finally, I will talk about N2 and A16 introduction. Our 2 nanometer and A16 technologies lead the industry in addressing the insatiable need for energy-efficient computing, and almost all the innovators are working with TSMC. We expect a number of the new tapeout for 2 nanometer technology in the first two years to be higher than both 3 nanometer and 5 nanometer in their first two years, fueled by both smartphone and HPC applications. N2 will deliver full-node performance and power benefit with 10 to 15 speed improvement at the same power or 20% to 30% power improvement at the same speed, and more than 15% chip density increase as compared with the N3E. N2 is well on track for volume production in second half of 2025 as scheduled, with a ramp profile similar to N3.

There is very strong demand for the leading nodes, as can be seen from the rapid increase in their contribution to Taiwan Semiconductor's revenue. For example, the 3nm node accounted for ~6% of the company's revenue in 2023 and roughly tripled to ~18% in 2024. Almost three quarters of revenue is attributed to nodes at 7nm and below.

Smaller transistors is not the only research and development focus for Taiwan Semiconductor. It has delivered impressive innovations in the field of inter-chip stacking technology. This allows for much better integrated systems in a single chip, which has multiple benefits: higher density, faster speeds, and lower power consumption. Taiwan Semiconductor's TSMC-SoIC™ technology is a 3D inter-chip stacking technology that enables integration of chiplets with multi-layers, multi-chip sizes, and multi-functions. This way, customers can receive complete Systems on a Chip (SoC) that improve performance and reduce costs and time to market. The company estimates this can result in roughly 10x speed performance, 190x higher bandwidth, and a 20x improvement in energy efficiency compared to traditional 3D ICs.

Perhaps unsurprisingly, high-performance computing (HPC) customers like NVIDIA and AMD (AMD), that derive enormous benefits from having their chips manufactured using cutting-edge technology, represented slightly more than half of revenue in 2024. HPC was also the platform that saw the largest year-over-year growth rate in 2024.

Unassailable Competitive Moat

Through a combination of factors, we believe Taiwan Semiconductor has built a very strong competitive moat. These include its vast economies of scale, technological leadership, high customer switching costs, and superb execution skills.

Competitors like Samsung Foundry have frequently had yield issues, and in some cases directly competes with potential customers. Similarly, Intel has experienced significant delays in its technology roadmap, and in some cases directly competes with potential customers. The competitive moat is reflected in the strong financials, including a gross profit margin approaching 60%, operating margins of almost 50%, and Returns on Invested Capital (ROIC) well above 20%. For comparison, Samsung's ROIC is ~8.5% while Intel's ROIC is currently negative.

Financials

Taiwan Semiconductor reports its monthly revenue, where we can see there has been a year-over-year growth acceleration. It is likely that part of the reason can be attributed to some customers trying to get chips imported before any potential tariff impact. As previously mentioned, revenue for the first quarter is slightly higher than expected by analysts, which is why we expect solid earnings results. What will be perhaps more important to how shares react will be guidance for the rest of the year.

Taking a longer-term view, it is clear Taiwan Semiconductor has delivered impressive growth over the past decade, despite some cyclicality. Its revenue has more than tripled, while Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) has only grown revenue by ~22%, and Intel has seen negative revenue growth.

Balance Sheet

Despite the high capital intensity that goes along with chip manufacturing, Taiwan Semiconductor has managed to keep a rock-solid balance sheet. It has a net cash position, and relied mostly on internally generated funds to finance its growth.

This has resulted in strong investment grade credit ratings, with S&P Global (SPGI) giving assigning 'AA-', while Moody's (MCO) gives the company an 'Aa3' rating. Taiwan Semiconductor has also proven disciplined when it comes to M&A.

Given its strong profitability and cash generation, Taiwan Semiconductor has actually been increasing its net cash position. This is impressive considering the significant capital expenditures and research and development investments the company has been making.

U.S.-China Economic Decoupling

Of course, the big risk on many investors' minds is the one Warren Buffett alluded to: the company's geographical location. While we still view a Chinese military invasion of Taiwan as a relatively low probability risk, there is no doubt the impact would be enormous. Unfortunately, the trade war, which is further decoupling the U.S. and Chinese economies, increases this risk, as China has now less to lose from potential economic sanctions.

This risk is difficult to quantify, and it is completely understandable that more risk-averse investors prefer to avoid investing in the company. That said, it is important to note that the potential impact on Taiwan Semiconductor's customers could also be massive, as they are extremely dependent on the company for their chips. Taiwan Semiconductor is mitigating the potential risk by opening fabs in the U.S., Europe, and Japan. Still, for the foreseeable future, most of its capacity, especially for leading nodes, will remain in Taiwan.

Future Outlook

From the monthly revenue reports, we know Taiwan Semiconductor is expected to report revenue that exceeds analyst expectations. Gross margins are expected to be slightly lower compared to Q42024, with the company warning that Q1 gross margin would see a decline of roughly 100 basis points due to ramp-up costs associated with N2 and its advanced packaging capabilities.

Looking further ahead, there are both headwinds and tailwinds to consider. The outlook for AI chips growth remains strong, with CEO C.C. Wei predicting an astounding mid-40% compound annual growth rates (CAGR) in the AI space alone over the next five years, and 20% revenue growth for all platforms:

Underpinned by our technology leadership and broad customer base, we now forecast the revenue growth from AI accelerators to approach a mid-40% CAGR for the five-year period, starting off the already higher base of 2024. We expect AI accelerators to be the strongest driver of our HPC platform growth and the largest contributor in terms of our overall incremental revenue growth in the next several years.

[...]

For the five-year period starting from 2024, we expect our long-term revenue growth to approach a 20% CAGR in U.S. dollar term, fueled by all four of our growth platforms which are smartphone, HPC, IoT and automotive.

We believe AI demand will continue to grow, but we are somewhat skeptical of such an elevated CAGR being sustainable for that long. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) is already rethinking its data center needs in various markets. Still, even if demand is reduced we believe that cancelled orders could potentially be replaced with new customers jumping at the opportunity to move from other foundries to work with Taiwan Semiconductor to benefit from the advantages of having chips manufactured with its cutting-edge technology.

Taiwan Semiconductor is certainly displaying confidence that customer demand will remain strong, as it continues with aggressive factory expansions, with high volume production expected soon in Arizona and Japan. Analysts appear optimistic as well, with Taiwan Semiconductor having more upward earnings and revenue revisions in the past three months, compared to downward revisions.

Valuation

If Taiwan Semiconductor and ASML were based in the U.S., we believe we would be talking about the magnificent nine, instead of the magnificent seven. In terms of growth, profit margins, and returns on invested capital, Taiwan Semiconductor is definitely one of the top companies in the world. Unlike most of the magnificent seven, Taiwan Semiconductor has a very reasonable valuation with a GAAP P/E (TTM) multiple of only ~17x, a PEG ratio of ~0.45x, and a net cash position equivalent to ~5% of its market capitalization.

Compared to its two most important customers, Apple and NVIDIA, and its most important supplier, ASML, Taiwan Semiconductor's valuation stands out as significantly cheaper.

Based on forward earnings estimated, shares appear even more undervalued, with the forward Price/Earnings multiple for FY2026 currently below 13x.

Risks

It is probably safe to say that at this point nobody really knows where tariffs will end up, as we have seen new developments and announcements almost daily the last few weeks. While we believe tariffs will likely be an overall negative for TSMC, there could be some hidden benefits. For example, it appears China will exempt U.S. chips from its retaliatory tariffs if they were manufactured outside the country. This could benefit fabless companies like AMD, NVIDIA, and Qualcomm, to the detriment of companies like Intel (INTC) and Texas Instruments (TXN).

The biggest risk remains potential Chinese military action on Taiwan. Still, there are other important risks to consider, including potential operational risks, including the N2 ramp and executing on its global fab expansion.

Tariffs, regulatory changes, and exchange rates can also present potential headwinds. Perhaps more worrying, U.S. consumers have been showing increasing signs of weakness, which could reduce discretionary purchases like smartphones and computers. There are many signs pointing to consumer weakness, including plunging consumer sentiment, and more credit card users opting to make only the minimum payments.

Conclusion

There is no doubt Taiwan Semiconductor is a wonderful company trading at a very attractive price. However, this comes with geopolitical risk, which is probably rising as the U.S. and Chinese economies decouple as a result of the escalating trade war. Still, should TSMC's factories be shut-off the effect would have a massive impact on the entire global economy, including many U.S. technology giants like NVIDIA and Apple.

We believe that, depending on each investor's risk tolerance, a position can be justified or not. While we fully acknowledge the risk that prompted Warren Buffett to sell, it is extremely difficult to find companies with the kind of fundamentals that Taiwan Semiconductor has, including potential annual revenue growth above 20%, and a very wide competitive moat, trading at less than 20 times earnings.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Braze: AI Acquisition Helps, But Avoid During Downturn
data.description:
Braze (BRZE) is a customer engagement platform that helps companies better interact with their customers. The company offers messaging solutions across several channels, such as email, SMS, and push notifications. Braze integrates with several data sources and uses this to help orchestrate marketing campaigns.

Even though the stock has been under a lot of pressure so far in 2025, I believe the path forward is still lower. Revenue growth is on a decelerating trend and profits are still minimal. Plus, with the prospects of a potential slowdown in consumer confidence, companies may opt to reduce their marketing spend, which would directly impact Braze.

Yes, valuation could be considered attractive at face value, but potential macro headwinds may cause growth to slow even more than expected. If that were the case, then valuation could see even more downside.

For now, I believe investors are best waiting for a better entry point. While difficult to time the market, this stock seems to be pricing in too much optimism given current market conditions.

Financial Update

During the company’s recent Q4 report, revenue grew 22.5% YOY to $160.4 million, coming in ~$4.7 million above expectations. Subscription revenue, which is the vast majority (~96%) of revenue, grew 22.2% YOY, slightly below the total revenue growth.

At face value, 22.5% revenue growth is very strong. However, the company’s growth has come under pressure over the past several quarters. Two years ago, Braze was growing over 40% YOY. That growth has since been nearly cut in half.

One of the biggest reasons behind this deceleration is increased customer churn. Management has talked about customers buying excess amount of subscriptions during the COVID-fueled growth era. As these contracts came up for renewal, many customers were reducing how much spend they were having with Braze.

Dollar-based net retention is a great metric that shows existing customer spending trends. For example, the 111% reported in Q4 means that existing customers spent 111% of the year-ago base, or 11% YOY growth.

This metric has been on a steady decline, for the reasons I noted above. With revenue trends continuing to decelerate, one should assume that the dollar-based net retention metric also comes under pressure.

With revenue having been under pressure, many investors would naturally look for a lift in profitability. Although profits have been improving at Braze, margins are still low.

During the most recent fiscal year, non-GAAP operating income was $0.0 million, or a breakeven 0% margin. Free cash flow for the year was $19.6 million, or ~3.3% margin. Improving profitability is something the company has talked a lot about in recent quarters, and yes, there is progress being made. However, the pace of profit improvements may be below what investors would like to see.

BRZE’s guidance for FY ’26 includes revenue of $686-691 million, or ~16% YOY growth. This once again assumes another year of revenue deceleration. Non-GAAP operating income of $25.5-29.5 million demonstrates an improved ~4% operating margin, and while this is nice to see, it remains low in absolute terms.

Braze also announced an agreement to acquire OfferFit, a company that provides AI-decisioning for personalization in CRM marketing. The $325 million purchase price is a large sum to pay for a company with a current market cap ~$3 billion. In addition, while this acquisition presents an opportunity to accelerate revenue growth, it comes at the cost of margin dilution.

While we expect to remain operating income positive for the year, inclusive of the proposed acquisition of OfferFit, the dilutive impact in fiscal year 2026 is likely to result in a temporary departure from the framework, with a return to a more meaningful year-over-year operating income expansion next year.

With the current macro environment being highly uncertain, there is potential that growth estimates will need to come down. Braze has already suggested that margins will be low once the OfferFit acquisition closes, so it appears the next revision of estimates will be low.

More Cautious Consumer Presents Headwinds

The recent headlines around potential tariff introductions and general concern about entering into a slower period of growth has weighed on the market for the past week. Among the market volatility, investors have started to take a “risk-off” approach, or sell stocks that are more exposed to macro volatility.

One area of enterprise spending that would likely slow down during a prolonged period of economic uncertainty would be marketing. This is frequently the easiest expense item that companies can flex up/down based on the buying environment. Are consumers more likely to have strong purchasing power? Then increase marketing efforts. Are consumers more likely to curtail their spending? Then slow down marketing efforts.

The Morning Consult offers a real-time indicator of consumer confidence, as seen in the chart above. While consumer confidence remains well above the low-point seen during COVID and the macro fears in 2022, the recent retracement in confidence is worrying.

Does this mean that companies will immediately slow down their marketing campaigns? Probably not. However, the negative trend may give companies more hesitation to increase their marketing efforts. And if lower consumer confidence in turn results in less spending, then history would suggest that marketing budgets, including the money spent on Braze’s solutions, may be at risk.

According to McKinsey & Company, one of their studies from December 2022 found that marketing budgets are an early expense item to be cut. This 2022 study was great timing given the weaker consumer confidence, as seen in the chart above.

With the economy still sending mixed signals, it’s no great surprise that companies are feeling skittish and cutting costs. Unfortunately, the marketing budget is often the first to go. In December 2022, we surveyed nearly three dozen chief marketing officers (CMOs) of major North American consumer companies. On average, they told us that their company boards demanded an 8 percent reduction in marketing expenditures over the previous 12 months. In some cases, marketing budgets were cut by as much as 10 to 20 percent. One large public company even slashed its marketing budget by more than 20 percent.

While I am not advocating that there are major red flags in terms of marketing budgets slowing down, but it is important to note the correlation between the 2022 slowdown and ensuing marketing cuts.

Valuation

Especially during volatile markets, software and technology companies can be nearly impossible to value. Many investors use the Rule of 40 score, or the sum of revenue growth and operating margin, to determine the relative attractiveness of a company.

Given BRZE’s guidance assumes a score of just <20, the stock likely deserves a discounted valuation.

During the prosperous periods of growth for Braze, the stock was trading at over 10x revenue. Currently, valuation stands at ~4.2x revenue.

As a rule of thumb, companies with a Rule of 40 score, or higher, often trade at >6x revenue, with leading companies trading over 10x. Some of the lower performing companies can trade at multiples as low as 2x revenue.

While I am not advocating that BRZE should be trading at 2x revenue, the decelerating revenue trends and low levels of profitability warrant a discounted multiple. Even at ~4.2x revenue, there seems to be more risk of the multiple coming down, especially if revenue growth becomes even more pressured this year due to macro challenges.

For now, I believe investors should avoid Braze and wait for improving macro signals. Buying a company with decelerating growth trends and a murky macro is always a difficult buy.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to my negative thesis is if consumer spending remains strong. If this holds true, then companies are likely to continue their marketing spend at normal levels, which would mean no pressure on Braze’s business. If revenue growth and profitability were to improve, the stock would likely experience a higher valuation, thus a higher stock price.

Additionally, companies often use a good deal of different marketing software vendors, with Braze being just one of them. While Braze includes many of the Fortune 1000 companies as their customers, competition from traditional vendors, such as Salesforce and Adobe, remains strong. Increased competition could result in customer churn.

data.title: Alphabet: I'd Load Up At $130
data.description:
Alphabet Inc. (GOOG)(GOOGL), better known as ‘Google,’ is one of the cheapest Magnificent 7 stocks on the market today. Trading at just 20 times earnings, it has multiples more typical of Chinese tech stocks than U.S. tech stocks. This is a remarkable fact because Google has a strong brand, a fast-growing cloud business, several products with over a billion users, and many other advantages. You’d think that a company with all this going for it would command a premium price, but Google is actually rather cheap.

One reason why Google stock is cheap right now is because it has a lot of capital expenditure [CAPEX] planned for this year. Like most big tech companies, Google is investing heavily in generative AI, and that entails a lot of spending. The company has guided for $75 billion in CAPEX in 2025, a full $17 billion more than Wall Street expected before the last earnings release. The big jump in expenses is a risk factor in itself; also, it isn’t likely that Google is turning profits on its generative AI offerings apart from those part of its cloud services segment: competitors OpenAI and Meta Platforms (META) do not expect their gen AI investments to become profitable for many years.

All of this is set against the backdrop of the current administration and its capricious economic policies. Donald Trump recently threatened to hit Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) with a 100% tariff if it doesn’t build a plant in the United States. The tax was never actually enacted, but the fact that Trump is threatening it while having enacted similar taxes just recently, suggests that the risk is a real one. If Trump did put a 100% tax on TSMC chips, it would materially impact Google, as Google depends on TSMC for manufacturing, as do most big tech companies. So the risk here is very real.

It’s for this reason that investors ought to demand a discount to Google’s recent valuation. The level of uncertainty in this market is off the charts, and you can’t rule out the possibility that another round of tariffs will make Google’s AI CAPEX even more costly.

At the same time, the company does have many things going for it. That’s undeniable. It has high margins, a great growth track record, a strong brand, and capable leadership. These are real assets. However, in this environment of heightened risk, investors ought to demand cheaper asset prices relative to earnings and cash flows, because many of the variables impacting valuation are completely unpredictable–I mean more unpredictable than usual!

When I last covered Google stock, I rated it a hold because its valuation had gotten stretched–this was last year when the price was at $202. In the article, I mentioned that I had held the stock before but had sold it near $170. Given that Google stock is cheaper than $170 now, and I’m still holding it, my relatively bearish tone might sound surprising. However, the level of risk impacting markets is simply much higher now than it was last year. Accordingly, I am maintaining my GOOG rating at hold, despite the stock having declined in price substantially since I last covered it.

Fundamentals

According to Seeking Alpha Quant, Google stock boasts the following fundamental metrics:

  • $8.04 in diluted EPS.

  • $24 in tangible book value [BV] per share.

  • $5.91 in free cash flow [FCF] per share.

Since 2021, Google’s earnings and BV have grown, while its FCF has flatlined. The sluggish FCF performance is a concern. If you look at Google’s cash flow statement, you can clearly see that its capital expenditures have been rising significantly since 2021. Much of this CAPEX spend is due to generative AI. It could well be that the company will have elevated CAPEX indefinitely due to needing more and more NVIDIA (NVDA) chips each year in order to stay ahead of competitors. In that scenario, the company’s future growth and margins will not be as good as those seen in the past. On the other hand, there is also the possibility that viable competitors to NVIDIA will emerge, and make running AI cheaper. In that scenario, Google has a very bright future ahead of it, but it’s hard to say which of these two scenarios will actually play out. Accordingly, it makes sense to want a lower earnings/FCF multiple on GOOG stock than the one the stock has historically had. Costs appear to be rising.

Market Risk and Uncertainty

Another risk factor for Google–and one that relates to the previous one discussed–is the rising uncertainty in the markets as a whole. Donald Trump’s tariff policies appear to be quite costly to companies on the surface of things, and they are extremely unpredictable. In the span of a week, we heard a report that Trump was considering a 90-day pause on tariffs, followed by a White House denial of the report, followed by an official White House announcement that the 90-day pause was going ahead. It’s simply not possible to predict sequences of events this volatile, and so, investors can only expect the unexpected. In an environment where relevant variables can’t be predicted, investors ought to demand a cheaper price.

Some of the unpredictable tariff impacts mentioned above are relevant to Google’s business. Trump has threatened to tariff TSMC semiconductors at 100%--that would dramatically ramp up Google’s expenses because Google buys many chips from TSMC. He has also threatened overall tariffs on Vietnam and China, where Pixel phones are manufactured. The tariff on China is still in effect, as it wasn’t reversed in Wednesday’s 90-day tariff pause. So there are parts of Google’s business that may be facing tariffs right now, and others that could face them in the future. So the market risks discussed in the previous paragraphs are also company-specific risks to Google.

Valuing Google

Looking at everything written above, there is a case to be made that Google is a very strong company facing increased risk and uncertainty. In light of this, how do we value the stock?

First, let’s look at the stock’s multiples, courtesy of Seeking Alpha Quant.

As you can see, Google trades at about 20 times earnings. This is below average for the Magnificent 7 and for the NASDAQ-100–that is a definite positive. However, this is an environment of far greater risk than that observed in the past. The fact that Google is cheaper than its peer group may not be enough. Investors may demand earnings multiples closer to 15 or even 10 in order to account for the uncertainty.

Now, let’s look at Google’s cash flows and earnings.

According to Seeking Alpha Quant, Google had $5.91 in FCF per share and $8.13 in EPS in the TTM period. Given the uncertainty present, we cannot forecast any near term growth. What we know is that $5.91 discounted at 5% is worth $118.20 and $8.13 discounted at 5% is $162.60. The average of the two is $140. The reason I average two metrics here is because it’s uncertain for how long Google’s elevated CAPEX will remain–it affects FCF immediately but EPS only gradually through depreciation. So I think $140 is the better measure to go with here: it accounts for the uncertainty about the CAPEX schedule. So $140 is the upper limit of what I’d pay for Google stock today. However, in this environment, it pays to err on the side of caution, so $130 is where I would, in fact, buy Google.

The Bottom-Line

We are in an environment of elevated uncertainty today. Nobody knows where the tariff cards will fall, and there are other risk factors to consider too. My conservative no-growth Google model technically spits out $140 as the fair value estimate for the stock, and I think investors would do fine over the very long term buying at that level. But personally, I’d err on the side of caution and pay $130.

data.title: Oakmark Bond Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio activity

The Fund entered the new year maintaining a modest overweight position to corporate credit and securitized debt and was underweight U.S. Treasurys versus its benchmark. That said, the majority of the Fund’s securitized position is in highly-rated agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS), and the corporate holdings are high credit quality with less than 20% in below investment grade. The Fund’s duration is approximately in-line with the benchmark at around six years and its yield-to-worst ended the first quarter at 5.4% with a current yield of 5.38%.

Excess returns for corporate credit in the first quarter were negative as investment grade corporate index spreads widened by 14 basis points. This weighed on performance as the Fund was moderately overweight in corporate credit versus its benchmark, while the Fund’s overweight to securitized debt added to performance given solid returns for that sector.

The Fund’s average weight to corporate credit was approximately 34.5% during the quarter versus about 24.2% for the benchmark. The Fund maintained an average overweight to securitized credit of roughly 11.7% versus about 1.2% for the benchmark. Security selection within corporate credit was slightly negative during the quarter and was partially offset by positive security selection in securitized debt.

Positioning and outlook

As mentioned in our first-quarter 2025 fixed income commentary, Today’s uncertainty gets priced into the market, the uncertainty driven by the recently announced tariffs and related news headlines has started to affect the fixed income market. For value investors like us, lowering prices signal emerging opportunity.

The repricing hasn’t been dramatic, but it matters. It’s how value in credit starts to rebuild. High yield (Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index) has widened to +375 as of this writing—almost 100 basis points off the tights (+281 on February 18, 2025) or a move of roughly 33%. Investment-grade BBB credit (Bloomberg U.S. BBB Corporate Index)—where most investment grade issuance sits—has widened to around +120, 25 basis points wider and 27% off the lows. Since the global financial crisis, spreads for high yields have ranged between 300–650 basis points, and spreads for investment grade have ranged between 100–200 for investment grade 84% of the time since March 20101. So, the recent shifts represent meaningful resets. They have the potential to generate not just total return, but also excess return, for long-term, patient capital.

In parts of high yield and BBB investment grade, spreads are finally starting to reflect real risks: business model fragility, capital structure leverage, and refinancing gaps. Securitized credit is repricing, too, especially in deals tied to consumer credit and commercial real estate where stress is more visible. These aren’t broad calls. We believe this market still rewards real credit work, not index exposure. But for the first time in a while, dislocations are surfacing that meet Oakmark’s bar for both return potential and margin of safety.

Finding opportunity in dislocations

One such dislocation emerged in auto asset-backed securities (auto ABS). We bought a newly issued BBB-rated tranche at a credit spread of 190 basis points, almost double what the market would have priced that level of risk even three weeks earlier. Such a large move was purely technical and disconnected from our view of the investment’s fundamental risk. In fact, the underlying auto loans would have to experience cumulative losses above 40% for our investment to see any write-down, while that type of loan pool typically exhibits losses below 20%. In our opinion, that healthy margin of safety against losses, combined with a rapid cheapening of credit spreads, created the kind of opportunity that fits our value investment thesis.

We also added exposure in Insulet Corp. (PODD) by participating in its inaugural senior unsecured bond deal used to refinance part of its 2026 convertible notes. This marked a meaningful evolution in PODD’s capital structure—from a growth-phase mix of converts and loans to something more befitting its scale and cash flow profile. We see it as a high-BB credit, anchored by a market leadership position in insulin delivery, strong free cash flow, and modest, declining leverage near 2.5x. Price talk came in at 7%, roughly 50 basis points wide of comparable high-quality healthcare credits–and 50 basis points wider than it would have been priced earlier in March before valuations in credit began to reset. That kind of mispricing doesn’t just offer near-term yield. It sets up longer term potential upside as our upgrade thesis plays out. These are still selective opportunities, not a broad-based buying spree. But the key difference is that we’re finally being paid to take risk. After a long period of restraint, we believe it’s time to start leaning in—carefully, selectively, and always with our bottom-up framework in mind.

Still early days

My goal isn’t to signal the “all-in” on buying fixed income risk today. If tariffs of the magnitude that have been recently discussed are fully enacted and sustained, we believe there will be plenty of opportunities to buy at even lower prices than today, especially in more cyclical corners of the market. Credit markets today aren’t broadly cheap, but they’re far more interesting than they were just a quarter ago. Spread widening has opened isolated entry points that finally offer fair compensation for real risk—especially in companies with reliable management teams and revenue streams. The reset likely isn’t complete and may even be in the early stages, but it’s in motion.

Our positioning today

With that in mind, our portfolio positioning reflects a cautious optimism. We’ve begun selectively increasing exposure to credit risk in areas where spreads offer meaningful compensation. That includes certain segments of high yield, BBB-rated corporates, and asset-backed securities with conservative structures and favorable technicals. At the same time, we’ve maintained strong liquidity and continue to hold a healthy allocation of U.S. Treasurys and short-duration instruments—both for defense and optionality. The opportunity set is growing, but patience remains a core part of our process as we assess further possible disruptions in our companies’ earnings streams.

How much disruption—and ultimately what gets priced into the market—will depend in large part on macro policy. At Oakmark, we don’t try to predict how these events will play out. We leave that to the few PhDs who claim they can. But we don’t ignore it either. Tariffs are real. They have the potential to shift earnings and raise default risk.

We do spend time focusing on how the companies in our portfolio can absorb that reset—through cash flow, balance sheet strength, and liquidity. That’s the key distinction. As debt investors, we don’t need to predict the exact outcome. But we do need a strong sense of the range—especially the downside—and the catalysts that could take us there.

Rather than betting on discrete macro outcomes, we focus on building portfolios of companies led by management teams equipped to navigate a wide range of scenarios. While others try to forecast the next macro squall, hoping to steer around it, we’re doing something different: picking what we believe are the right captain and the right boat. We want leadership that has proven it can navigate calm seas and rough waters alike and businesses that are built to absorb volatility, not avoid it. If we trust the captain and know the vessel is sound, we don’t need to predict every storm. We just need to stay on course.

That means we position our portfolio to hold up in tightening or easing cycles, recession or recovery, inflation or deflation. And we focus on securities where we think the embedded risk premium compensates us for real uncertainty—not headlines. When dislocations emerge, we lean in where the downside is well understood, and the upside still reflects investor fear. That mindset kept us disciplined during years of aggressive pricing, and we believe it will now help us step back into a market that looks more compelling.

In short, the market feels different—not because uncertainty is new, but because it’s finally being acknowledged and priced. Tariffs may be the catalyst, and we are monitoring their effects on the market. But what is notable is the shift in sentiment from complacency to caution, from greed to fear. And that shift creates opportunity for disciplined value buyers.

Through it all, our process doesn’t change. We stay grounded in fundamentals. We don’t react to noise. We invest in management teams and capital structures built to handle a range of outcomes. And when we find mispriced risk with a wide margin of safety, we act.
